name: African Woman with Basket
description:
This 20-centime stamp was issued by the Republic of Rwanda in 1975 as part of an eight-stamp issue commemorating THEMABELGA 1975, a thematic philatelic exhibition held in Brussels, Belgium. StampWorld lists the issue as “International Stamp Exhibition ‘THEMABELGA 1975’ – Belgium – African Costumes”, issued on December 8, 1975, with perforation 13½ and design credited to O. Bonnevalle.

The stamp shows an African woman carrying a large basket on her head, rendered in a clean, illustrative style with strong white space and a thin red frame. The vertical text at left, “THEMABELGA 1975,” identifies the exhibition connection, while the large “RWANDAISE” at the bottom identifies the issuing country in French. A secondary philatelic source describes the 20c value specifically as “African Woman with Basket on the head.”

THEMABELGA 1975 was part of a wider culture of international stamp exhibitions, where countries issued commemorative stamps not only to mark the event but also to present visually appealing themes to collectors. A Belgian source describes THEMABELGA as a World Exhibition of Thematic Philately held in Brussels from December 13 to 21, 1975, focused on thematic collecting—organizing stamps by subject rather than strictly by country or chronology. Rwanda’s choice of African costumes fit that thematic-philately context well: the issue was visually collectible, culturally recognizable, and organized around a subject rather than a political event.

Historically, this stamp comes from post-independence Rwanda. Rwanda had become independent from Belgium in 1962, after years as part of the Belgian-administered Ruanda-Urundi territory. By 1975, Rwanda was a young republic using stamps, like many newly independent nations, to circulate images of national and regional identity to an international audience. Because stamps traveled through mail and entered collectors’ albums worldwide, they often functioned as miniature cultural ambassadors.

The subject of traditional dress is also significant. Clothing, beadwork, baskets, head-carrying practices, and body ornamentation were frequent themes in mid-20th-century African stamp issues. These images could celebrate cultural heritage, but they also need to be read with some caution: many “costume” issues were created for international collectors and sometimes presented generalized or idealized views of African identity rather than documenting a specific named community, region, or garment tradition.

name: Stroje Łowickie
description:
This 60 groszy stamp was issued by Poland in 1969 as part of the Folk Costumes series. The inscription at the top, “Stroje Łowickie,” means Łowicz costumes — traditional dress from the Łowicz region of central Poland, near Łódź. 

The image shows a dancing couple in traditional Łowicz dress. The woman’s striped skirt is especially important: the brightly colored striped textile, often called pasiak, became one of the most recognizable elements of Polish folk costume. Polish textile traditions describe pasiaki as striped woolen fabrics whose colors became increasingly elaborate in the 19th century as dyeing methods improved.

Łowicz dress is one of the best-known regional costume traditions in Poland. Google Arts & Culture describes Łowicz costumes as among the most representative costumes of central Poland and notes that their colors changed over time: late 19th-century fabrics often used red backgrounds, later shifting toward orange, and by the 1930s, synthetic aniline dyes brought cooler colors such as green, blue, violet, and gray.

Historically, folk costumes in Poland were not everyday casual clothing in the modern sense. They were often worn for religious celebrations, seasonal festivals, weddings, and other important community events. A cultural history source on Polish folk dress describes these costumes as elegant garments connected to rural identity, ritual life, and regional belonging. This matters for the stamp: it does not just show clothing, but a visual shorthand for local identity, celebration, and cultural continuity.

The Łowicz region itself is part of Mazowsze in central Poland, west of Warsaw. A Polish folklore source describes the region as historically connected to the old Kingdom of Łowicz, where the local costume developed rapidly from simpler colors into the vivid “rainbow” palette now associated with the area. The stamp’s design emphasizes that color and movement by showing the couple dancing rather than standing formally.

This issue also fits a broader mid-20th-century pattern in postage design. Many countries used stamps to present national culture, regional identity, folk art, architecture, and traditional dress to both domestic audiences and international collectors. In Poland’s case, the Folk Costumes series presented different regional costumes as part of a shared national cultural heritage.

name: Ernest E. Just
thumbnail: United States — Ernest E. Just, Black Heritage, 1996-Thumb.webp
largeImage: United States — Ernest E. Just, Black Heritage, 1996-Medium.webp
description:
This 32-cent United States commemorative stamp was issued on February 1, 1996, in Washington, D.C., as part of the USPS Black Heritage series. It honors Ernest Everett Just, a pioneering African American biologist best known for his work in marine biology, embryology, fertilization, and cell development. The stamp is cataloged as Scott #3058 and was the 19th stamp in the Black Heritage series.

The design is based on a 1912 photograph by Robert Scurlock, a Washington, D.C. photographer known for documenting prominent Black Americans. This was a notable design shift for the Black Heritage series: it was the first stamp in the series to use a photograph rather than a painted portrait. The stamp was designed by Richard Sheaff of Norwood, Massachusetts, printed by the Banknote Corporation of America, and produced by lithography in panes of 20.

Ernest Everett Just was born in Charleston, South Carolina, in 1883. He graduated from Dartmouth College in 1907, then joined Howard University, where he taught and later became a major figure in the biology department. While at Howard, Just came into contact with University of Chicago zoologist Frank R. Lillie, who helped open the path for his advanced research and graduate study. Just earned his Ph.D. in zoology from the University of Chicago in 1916.

Just’s scientific reputation was built largely through his work at the Marine Biological Laboratory in Woods Hole, Massachusetts. There, he studied fertilization and early development in marine organisms such as sea urchins and marine worms. His first major publication, in 1912, showed that the point where sperm enters the egg helps determine the first cleavage plane during cell division — a fundamental observation in embryology.

His broader legacy rests on his insistence that scientists study living cells under conditions as close to natural as possible. Rather than focusing only on isolated cell parts, Just argued for the importance of the whole cell and especially the cell surface in development. Howard University’s biology department describes his primary legacy as recognizing the fundamental role of the cell surface in the development of organisms.

The stamp’s place in the Black Heritage series is significant. USPS began the series in 1978 to recognize the achievements of individual African Americans, and by honoring Just in 1996, the Postal Service placed a Black scientist alongside better-known cultural, political, and civil rights figures. His inclusion also reflects a broader recovery of his reputation. For decades after his death, Just’s work was less widely remembered than it deserved to be, in part because of the racial barriers he faced in American science. Later scholarship, especially Kenneth R. Manning’s biography Black Apollo of Science, helped restore attention to Just’s scientific importance.

name: Cymothoe sangaris (Blood-Red Glider)
thumbnail: Central African Republic — Cymothoe sangaris, Butterflies, 1961-Thumb.webp
largeImage: Central African Republic — Cymothoe sangaris, Butterflies, 1961-Medium.webp
description:
This 0.50 franc stamp was issued by the Central African Republic as part of its Butterflies series. The subject is Cymothoe sangaris, commonly known as the Blood-red Glider or Blood-red Glider butterfly. 

The timing is important. The Central African Republic had only just emerged as a new republic. Formerly the French colony of Ubangi-Shari, it became the Central African Republic on December 1, 1958, and gained full independence from France on August 13, 1960. The U.S. Office of the Historian also records August 13, 1960 as the date independence from French Equatorial Africa was proclaimed.

Because of that, this stamp belongs to the earliest period of the country’s independent postal identity. Like many stamps from newly independent African states, it presented local natural life to both domestic users and international collectors. Rather than focusing on political leaders or state symbols, the Butterflies series used vivid wildlife imagery to communicate biodiversity, beauty, and national distinctiveness.

The butterfly shown, Cymothoe sangaris, is a member of the Nymphalidae family. Modern butterfly references identify it as the Blood Red Glider and place its range across West and Central Africa, including Cameroon, Gabon, Congo, Angola, the Central African Republic, and the Democratic Republic of Congo. Its habitat is described as wetter forest in good condition, and its larvae feed on plants in the Rinorea genus.

name: Apollo 17
description:
This 2-cent stamp was issued by Liberia in 1973 as part of a commemorative issue marking the Apollo 17 Moon mission. 

The stamp design combines several symbols of the Apollo 17 mission: an astronaut working on the lunar surface, the Moon’s gray terrain, the Apollo 17 mission emblem, and a stylized Liberian flag band at the bottom. The mission emblem shown on the stamp includes the names Cernan, Evans, and Schmitt, referring to the Apollo 17 crew: Eugene A. Cernan, Ronald E. Evans, and Harrison H. “Jack” Schmitt. 

NASA identifies Apollo 17 as the Apollo program’s last lunar landing mission and the first to include an astronaut-scientist; Schmitt was a geologist and became the first scientist to walk on the Moon.

Apollo 17 launched on December 7, 1972, and splashed down on December 19, 1972. The lunar landing site was the Taurus-Littrow Valley, chosen because scientists hoped it could provide both older and younger lunar rocks than those collected by earlier Apollo missions and Soviet Luna sample returns. The mission’s scientific focus made it a fitting subject for countries issuing space-themed stamps in the early 1970s: Apollo 17 represented both the climax of the Moon race and the growing scientific purpose of lunar exploration.

The stamp also reflects Liberia’s long-running relationship with American history and iconography. Liberia was founded in the 19th century through the activities of the American Colonization Society, and it declared independence in 1847. The U.S. State Department notes that the United States established diplomatic relations with Liberia in 1862 and maintained strong ties with the country for many decades. Because of that historical relationship, Liberia’s choice to commemorate a major American space achievement is not surprising; Liberian stamps from this period often appealed to international topical collectors, especially those interested in space, aviation, transport, and world events.

name: Madonna and Child by Vivarini
thumbnail: Cayman Islands — Madonna and Child by Vivarini, Christmas 1969, 1969-Thumb.webp
largeImage: Cayman Islands — Madonna and Child by Vivarini, Christmas 1969, 1969-Medium.webp
description:
This ¼-cent stamp was issued by the Cayman Islands as part of its Christmas 1969 issue. The design reproduces The Virgin and Child by Alvise Vivarini, a Venetian Renaissance painter active in the late 15th century. 

The painting shown on this stamp is generally associated with Vivarini’s The Virgin and Child, dated to about 1483. The National Gallery in London lists a painting by Alvise Vivarini under that title, made in oil on wood, signed, and now part of its main collection. Vivarini belonged to an important Venetian painting family from Murano, and his work sits within the religious image-making tradition of Renaissance Venice. On the stamp, the image is framed in a bright red-orange border, turning a devotional Renaissance painting into a compact Christmas commemorative.

The subject itself reflects a long Christian artistic tradition: Mary, or the Madonna, is shown with the infant Christ. Christmas stamps across many Commonwealth and British territories often used Nativity scenes, Madonnas, angels, and European religious paintings, especially in the mid-20th century. For the Cayman Islands, this was a way to mark the holiday using imagery that would be recognizable to both local postal users and international collectors.

The political context is also useful. In 1969, the Cayman Islands were still a British Crown Colony during the reign of Queen Elizabeth II, whose royal cypher appears in the upper-right corner of the stamp. Cayman’s government history notes that after Jamaica became independent in 1962, Cayman chose to remain a Crown Colony governed by an administrator reporting directly to Westminster. That colonial status is visible in the stamp’s design through the E II R royal cypher and the English country inscription.

name: Child with Plant and Rainbow
thumbnail: Israel — Child with Plant and Rainbow, Arbor Day, 1975-Thumb.webp
largeImage: Israel — Child with Plant and Rainbow, Arbor Day, 1975-Medium.webp
description:
This 0.01 lira stamp was issued by Israel in 1975 as part of an Arbor Day / Tu B’Shvat stamp issue. The design shows a child holding a young plant beneath a rainbow, with flowers and grass in the foreground. The vertical text reads “Israel arbor day” in English, Hebrew, and Arabic, reflecting Israel’s multilingual postal design conventions.

The issue is connected to Tu B’Shvat, the Jewish “New Year of the Trees.” The National Library of Israel identifies a 1975 poster advertising Tu B’Shvat / Arbor Day stamps issued on January 14, 1975, corresponding to 2 Shvat 5735. The listed denominations were 0.01, 0.35, and 2.00 lira, and the top stamps on the poster depict children celebrating Tu B’Shvat.

Tu B’Shvat has ancient roots in Jewish law as a date used for calculating the age of fruit trees, but in modern Israel it became strongly associated with tree planting, land renewal, ecological education, and children’s participation in nature-centered celebrations. The child holding a plant on this stamp fits that modern civic and educational version of the holiday: the design is less about ritual observance and more about growth, renewal, and the relationship between children, land, and future stewardship.

The rainbow is also important visually. It gives the stamp a hopeful, almost poster-like quality, using simplified shapes and bright color fields rather than detailed naturalism. This makes the stamp feel very much of its 1970s graphic-design moment: flat forms, saturated color, minimal facial detail, and a strong symbolic composition. The design communicates optimism and environmental care quickly, even at a very small size.
